step.title: Step 40
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> SPACE -> LEFT -> SPACE -> LEFT -> SPACE -> LEFT -> SPACE -> LEFT -> SPACE -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 10 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, SPACE, LEFT, SPACE, LEFT, SPACE, LEFT, SPACE, LEFT, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 305, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 304 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 10.1x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 152 actions returned the board to a state already seen 152 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 152 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over! The message says "The game is over." But it also says "You are still on the same level." and "Current state: step 305, level 1."
Wait, but the message also says "The game is over." This might mean the timer ran out and the game reset.
Looking at the current image, the eyes are at (44, 19) and (44, 39). This is the initial position of the eyes! So the game has reset.
This is the same state as the initial frame. The eyes are back at (44, 19) and (44, 39).
So the game reset because the timer ran out. I need to start over and try a different approach.
Let me think about what I learned from the previous run:
1. The eyes can move UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT within the black shape.
2. The left eye can move left from col 19 to col 14, and right from col 14 to col 44.
3. The right eye can move left from col 39 to col 34, and right from col 34 to col 44.
4. When both eyes are in the same column (col 44), they move together (UP/DOWN).
5. SPACE doesn't complete the level at any position I tried.
6. MOUSE clicks don't do anything.
7. The game resets when the timer runs out.
What if the goal is not about moving the eyes at all, but about something else? Like, the goal is to make the eyes change color? Or to make the eyes disappear?
Wait, I just noticed something. In the current image, the eyes are at (44, 19) and (44, 39). But in the initial image, the eyes were also at (44, 19) and (44, 39). So the game has reset to the initial state.
But wait, the current image shows the eyes at (44, 19) and (44, 39), which is the initial position. So the game has reset.
Let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to move the eyes to a specific position where they're at the same row AND same column? But the eyes can't overlap, so this is not possible.
What if the goal is to move the eyes to a position where they're at the same row? Like, both eyes at row 32?
Currently, the eyes are at rows 44 and 44. So they're already at the same row! But pressing SPACE at this position didn't complete the level (as I observed earlier).
Wait, but I pressed SPACE at (44, 19) and (44, 39) earlier, and it didn't complete the level. So maybe the goal is not about pressing SPACE at the initial position.
What if the goal is to move the eyes to a different position where they're at the same row? Like, both eyes at row 32?
But the eyes' vertical movement is constrained by their column. At col 19, the left eye can move between rows 14 and 44. At col 39, the right eye can move between rows 39 and 54. So the eyes can't be at the same row unless they're at col 44 (where they move together).
Wait, but when both eyes are at col 44, they move together. So pressing UP from (44, 44) and (44, 44) would move both eyes up. But the eyes can't be at the same position (44, 44), so this is not possible.
Actually, I think the eyes can't be at the same row unless they're in the same column. And when they're in the same column, they move together, so they maintain their relative positions.
OK, I'm really stuck. Let me try a completely different approach.
What if the goal is to move the eyes to a position where they're at the corners of the black shape? Like, top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right?
The black shape has corners at approximately:
- Top-left: (9, 14)
- Top-right: (9, 44)
- Bottom-left: (49, 14)
- Bottom-right: (54, 44)
I've tried moving the eyes to these positions, but pressing SPACE didn't complete the level.
What if the goal is to move the eyes to a position where they're at the center of the black shape? Like, (32, 32)?
But the eyes can't be at (32, 32) because the black shape doesn't extend to that position.
Actually, let me look at the black shape more carefully. The black shape is a face-like shape with two eye sockets. The left eye socket is at approximately rows 14-49, cols 14-24. The right eye socket is at approximately rows 39-54, cols 39-44.
Wait, but the eyes can move to positions outside these sockets. For example, the left eye can move to col 44, which is outside the left eye socket.
So the eyes can move anywhere within the black shape, not just within their respective eye sockets.
OK, I'm really stuck. Let me try one more thing. What if the goal is to move the eyes to a position where they're at the boundary between the yellow and orange regions? Like, at col 32?
Currently, the eyes are at cols 19 and 39. The left eye can move right from col 19 to col 44, and the right eye can move left from col 39 to col 34. So the eyes can meet at col 32?
Let me try moving the left eye right and the right eye left to see if they can meet at col 32.
Actually, I observed earlier that when I pressed RIGHT from (14, 14) and (39, 44), the left eye moved right to col 19, but the right eye stayed at col 44. So the right eye didn't move left. This suggests that the right eye's horizontal movement is constrained.
But when I pressed LEFT from (9, 44) and (39, 44), the left eye moved left to col 39, and the right eye moved right to col 49. Wait, that's not right. Let me check the output again.
From the previous run:
- LEFT 1: [(9, 39), (39, 49)]
So when I pressed LEFT from (9, 44) and (39, 44), the left eye moved left to col 39, and the right eye moved right to col 49. But the right eye was at col 44, and it moved to col 49. So the right eye moved right, not left.
This is strange. When I press LEFT, the left eye moves left and the right eye moves right. When I press RIGHT, the left eye moves right and the right eye moves left.
So the eyes move in opposite directions horizontally. When I press LEFT, the left eye moves left and the right eye moves right (spreading apart). When I press RIGHT, the left eye moves right and the right eye moves left (coming together).
But earlier, I observed that when I pressed RIGHT from (14, 14) and (39, 44), the left eye moved right to col 19, but the right eye stayed at col 44. So the right eye didn't move left. This suggests that the right eye's horizontal movement is constrained when it's at col 44.
OK, so the eyes' horizontal movement is:
- LEFT: Left eye moves left, right eye moves right (spreading apart).
- RIGHT: Left eye moves right, right eye moves left (coming together).
But the eyes' movement is constrained by the black shape. The left eye can only move left to col 14, and the right eye can only move right to col 49 (or 54?).
Let me try pressing LEFT from the initial position (44, 19) and (44, 39) to see if the eyes spread apart.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (REVISED):
- Game reset! Eyes back at initial position (44, 19) and (44, 39)
- Previous run: 304 actions, no completion, timer ran out
- Key insight: LEFT spreads eyes apart (left eye left, right eye right), RIGHT brings them together
- SPACE never completed level at any position tried
- Need completely new hypothesis about goal
Goal model: Maybe goal is to spread eyes to maximum separation? Or bring them to minimum separation? Or move them to specific corners?
Plan: Try LEFT to spread eyes apart from initial position. Check if this completes level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game reset! Eyes at (44, 19) and (44, 39)
# Try LEFT to spread eyes apart
for i in range(10):
    result = action(['LEFT'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
    positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
    print(f"LEFT {i+1}: {positions}")
    if result.get('done'):
        print(f"  DONE!")
        break
    # Check if eyes stopped moving (no_impact)
    if not result.get('board_changed', True):
        print(f"  No board change, stopping")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
